ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'milk tea' class (FOODON_03301103); no specific Taiwanese black tea latte entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers tea-based beverages broadly; no specific milk tea or black tea latte code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for black tea latte or Taiwanese milk tea identified in the clinical terminology.

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium-sized cup of black tea latte as typically served at KOI Thé and similar Taiwanese bubble tea chains in Singapore, approximately 500 ml.

nutrient_source.title: Milk Tea (with whole milk and sugar) — estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data: Brewed Black Tea (FDC ID 171917) and Whole Milk (FDC ID 171265), combined in approximate 60:40 ratio with added sugar at standard sweetness level
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Taiwanese black tea latte exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Values estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for brewed black tea (FDC 171917) and whole milk (FDC 171265) in a 60:40 tea-to-milk ratio, with approximately 8 g sugar per 100 ml added at standard sweetness. This is consistent with published compositional analyses of milk tea beverages. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of sweetened milk tea estimated from analogous beverage: sweetened dairy-based tea drink; reference GI of sucrose (GI 65) and whole milk (GI 27–34) combined with tea base; analogous estimate anchored to University of Sydney GI Database entries for milk-based beverages and sugar-sweetened drinks
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Taiwanese black tea latte found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on the blended contribution of brewed black tea (GI effectively ~0 for the tea fraction), whole milk (GI ~27), and added sucrose (GI ~65), weighted by carbohydrate contribution. Glycemic load per 500 ml serving = (55 × 62.5 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 34.4. This is consistent with published GI estimates for sweetened milk tea beverages in the literature.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~110 mg per 500 ml serving)
hypertension.guidance: A medium black tea latte contains approximately 110 mg sodium per serving, which is low and unlikely to significantly impact blood pressure; however, individuals with hypertension should monitor total daily sugar and caloric intake from sweetened beverages.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High added sugar content and low-medium glycemic index (~55) resulting in a high glycemic load (~34) per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit or avoid standard-sweetness black tea lattes due to the high glycemic load (~34 per 500 ml serving) driven by approximately 59 g of sugar per serving; requesting reduced or zero sugar is strongly advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from whole milk and high added sugar contributing to caloric density
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of full-sugar black tea lattes contributes to excess caloric and saturated fat intake; individuals with cardiovascular disease should opt for reduced-sugar versions and consider low-fat milk alternatives to limit saturated fat to below 4.5 g per serving.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Black Tea Latte (Medium), known in Chinese as 鮮奶紅茶 (xiān nǎi hóng chá), is a popular Taiwanese-style milk tea beverage made by combining strongly brewed black tea with fresh whole milk, typically sweetened with cane sugar. It is a signature offering at Taiwanese tea chains such as KOI Thé, widely available across Singapore. The drink has a smooth, creamy texture with a robust tea flavour balanced by the natural sweetness of fresh milk and added sugar. It is served cold by default but can be ordered hot. A medium serving is approximately 500 ml. Nutritionally, a medium cup provides roughly 360 kcal, 8 g protein, 7.5 g fat, and 62.5 g carbohydrate, of which approximately 59 g is sugar. The high sugar content gives this beverage a low glycemic index (~55) but a high glycemic load (~34) per serving, making it a significant concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The beverage contains dairy (milk allergen) and is vegetarian and halal-friendly. It is not suitable for vegans or those who are lactose intolerant. Black tea provides trace amounts of flavonoids and caffeine. Clinically, this drink should be treated as a high-sugar beverage and consumed in moderation; reduced-sugar or sugar-free options are recommended for health-conscious consumers.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a KOI black tea latte?
How much sugar is in a medium milk tea?
What is the GI of milk tea?
Black tea latte nutrition facts Singapore

confidence_reason: No direct nutritional entry for Taiwanese black tea latte (medium, KOI Thé style) was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values were estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for brewed black tea and whole milk in a 60:40 ratio with standard sugar addition. GI was estimated analogously from University of Sydney GI Database reference values for milk-based and sugar-sweetened beverages. Confidence is medium due to the estimation approach.
notes: Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data (brewed black tea FDC 171917 + whole milk FDC 171265) in a 60:40 ratio with ~8 g sugar per 100 ml. No direct branded entry for KOI Thé black tea latte was available in any approved source. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA component sources used for this estimation. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA component sources used for this estimation. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA component sources used for this estimation. Trans fat is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA whole milk data (trace amounts below detection threshold). Dietary fibre is 0.0 — naturally zero as brewed tea and milk contain no dietary fibre. The glycemic load per serving is calculated as (GI 55 × 62.5 g carbohydrate per 500 g serving) / 100 = 34.4. This record should be updated if HPB Singapore Foodo or a peer-reviewed compositional study for KOI Thé or equivalent Taiwanese milk tea becomes available. The input description '中-鮮奶红茶' confirms this is the medium-size fresh milk black tea variant. Taiwanese cuisine multilingual naming rules applied: Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names provided. Note: this food is Taiwanese cuisine sold in Singapore; Singaporean multilingual naming rules have been applied as the food is served in Singapore context.
